# Feature Specification: M0.1 -- Walking on Tiles
*Generated by Game Studio Sub-Agents v1.0*

## Feature Overview
**Feature Name**: Tile-Based Player Movement and Map Rendering
**Project**: Nookstead
**Engine**: Phaser.js 3 + Next.js 16
**Priority**: High (Critical Path -- all other milestones depend on this)
**Estimated Effort**: 2 weeks (10 working days)
**Dependencies**: None (first milestone)
**Owner**: Mechanics Developer
**Data Impact**: session_start, session_end, performance_sample, asset_load_time

## Market Justification
*Analysis by Market Analyst Agent*

**Player Demand**: Smooth, responsive tile-based movement is the baseline expectation for every 2D pixel art game. Stardew Valley and Pokemon set the standard. Any perception of jankiness or lag will immediately undermine player trust.
**Competitive Analysis**: All competitors (Stardew Valley, Animal Crossing, Palia) offer smooth, responsive character movement as table stakes. Palia launched with movement bugs and was heavily criticized.
**Market Impact**: This is foundational -- not a differentiator, but a disqualifier if done poorly. Must feel polished even in prototype.
**Priority Rationale**: Critical path. Every subsequent feature (multiplayer, dialogue, farming) requires a working map and movement system.

## Problem Statement
Players need to navigate a 2D pixel-art town rendered from Tiled JSON maps using Phaser.js 3, embedded within a Next.js 16 web application. The player character must move smoothly using keyboard (desktop) or touch (mobile) input, with proper collision against buildings, water, and objects. The camera must follow the player with smooth scrolling.

## User Stories
- **As a player on desktop**, I want to **move my character with WASD or arrow keys** so that **I can explore the town freely and naturally**
- **As a player on mobile**, I want to **use a virtual joystick to move** so that **I have precise directional control on touchscreen**
- **As a player**, I want to **see my character walking through a detailed pixel art town** so that **I feel immersed in the world of Nookstead**
- **As a player**, I want to **be unable to walk through walls, water, or buildings** so that **the world feels solid and real**

## Acceptance Criteria
### Must Have
- [ ] Phaser.js 3 game instance renders within Next.js page without SSR errors
- [ ] Tiled JSON map (64x64 tiles, 16x16 tile size) loads and renders correctly with all layers
- [ ] Player sprite renders with 4-directional walk animation (up/down/left/right, 4 frames each)
- [ ] Player moves at consistent speed with keyboard input (WASD + arrows)
- [ ] Collision layer prevents walking through buildings, water, objects
- [ ] Camera follows player with smooth scrolling and edge clamping
- [ ] 60 FPS on desktop Chrome, 30 FPS on mobile Chrome
- [ ] Map loads in < 3 seconds on desktop, < 5 seconds on mobile

### Should Have
- [ ] Virtual joystick for mobile touch input
- [ ] Player idle animation when not moving
- [ ] Smooth camera deadzone (player can move slightly before camera follows)
- [ ] Map boundary collision (player cannot walk off the map)

### Could Have
- [ ] Day/night tint overlay (static for now, based on hardcoded time)
- [ ] Depth sorting for objects (player walks behind tree canopies)
- [ ] Minimap overlay

## Functional Requirements
### Input Specifications
- **Desktop**: WASD and Arrow keys for 4-directional movement. Diagonal movement allowed (8-directional).
- **Mobile**: Virtual joystick (left thumb area) for analog directional input
- **Input Behavior**: Hold key/joystick for continuous movement. Release to stop. No tap-to-move.

### Output Specifications
- **Visual Feedback**: Player sprite animates in direction of movement. Idle animation when stationary. Sprite faces last movement direction.
- **Audio Feedback**: Footstep sounds on grass (soft) and stone path (hard). Different sounds per surface type. (Could Have for M0.1)
- **Haptic Feedback**: None (browser limitation)

### State Management
- **Initial State**: Player spawns at designated spawn point on map. Camera centered. Idle animation.
- **Active States**: IDLE (standing, facing last direction), WALKING (moving, directional animation), BLOCKED (attempting to move into collision)
- **Transition Conditions**: IDLE -> WALKING on input. WALKING -> IDLE on input release. WALKING -> BLOCKED on collision (sprite does not move, animation may play "push" or stop).

## Technical Requirements
### Performance Constraints
- **CPU Impact**: < 5% CPU on desktop; < 15% on mobile for movement + collision checks
- **Memory Usage**: < 50 MB for loaded map + player sprite sheet + tile atlas
- **Rendering Cost**: < 100 draw calls (Phaser batch rendering for tiles)

### Data Structures
```typescript
// Player entity
interface PlayerEntity {
  sprite: Phaser.GameObjects.Sprite;
  speed: number;              // 100 pixels/sec (tunable)
  direction: 'up' | 'down' | 'left' | 'right';
  state: 'idle' | 'walking';
  position: { x: number; y: number };  // World position in pixels
}

// Tilemap layers
interface TilemapConfig {
  ground: Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer;
  paths: Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer;
  shadows: Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer;
  objects_lower: Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer;
  objects_upper: Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer;  // Rendered above player
  collision: Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer;       // Invisible, collision only
}
```

### Integration Points
- **Systems Used**: Phaser Arcade Physics (collision), Phaser Tilemap (rendering), Phaser Input (keyboard/touch)
- **Events Triggered**: `player_moved(x, y, direction)`, `player_idle()`, `zone_entered(zone_id)`
- **Events Consumed**: None (M0.1 is standalone)

## Balancing Parameters
### Tunable Values
- **Player Walk Speed**: 100 px/sec - Range: 60-150 - Purpose: Controls pace of exploration. Too fast feels twitchy; too slow feels sluggish.
- **Camera Deadzone Width**: 48 px - Range: 0-96 - Purpose: How far player can move before camera follows. Larger = more stable camera.
- **Camera Lerp Factor**: 0.1 - Range: 0.05-0.3 - Purpose: Smoothness of camera follow. Lower = smoother but more "floaty."

### Balancing Goals
Movement should feel responsive but not twitchy. The character should feel "grounded" in the world. Camera should never feel jarring. Reference: Stardew Valley's movement speed and camera behavior.

## Edge Cases & Error Handling
### Edge Cases
- **Player at map boundary**: Camera stops scrolling; player cannot move past map edges
- **Diagonal movement into corner**: Player slides along wall rather than stopping (wall-slide behavior)
- **Tab focus lost**: Game pauses input processing when browser tab is not focused
- **Browser resize**: Phaser.Scale.FIT re-centers the canvas without reloading the scene
- **Mobile orientation change**: Canvas resizes; joystick repositions

### Error Conditions
- **Tilemap fails to load**: Show loading error with retry button. Log asset_load_time event with error flag.
- **WebGL context lost**: Fall back to Canvas renderer. Log client_error event.
- **Low FPS detected**: Reduce rendering quality (disable shadows layer, reduce camera lerp frequency). Log performance_sample with FPS alert.

## User Experience Considerations
### Accessibility
- Keyboard input works without mouse (fully keyboard-navigable)
- Movement speed is configurable in settings (for players with motor impairments)
- High contrast mode reduces visual noise for visibility

### Learning Curve
- Movement is intuitive (standard WASD/arrow controls)
- No tutorial needed for basic movement
- Virtual joystick appears automatically on touch devices

### Player Feedback
- Walk animation confirms movement is happening
- Collision response (sprite stops) confirms world is solid
- Camera follow confirms spatial awareness

## Analytics Integration
*Designed by Data Scientist Agent*

### Telemetry Events
- **session_start**: When Phaser game boots and player enters map
- **performance_sample**: Every 30 seconds -- FPS, memory, draw calls
- **asset_load_time**: Time to load tilemap JSON and tile atlases
- **client_error**: Any JavaScript error during gameplay
- **player_movement_sample**: Every 30 seconds -- position for heatmap (preparing for M0.2+)

### Performance Baselines
This milestone establishes performance baselines for all future milestones:
- Desktop FPS target: 60
- Mobile FPS target: 30
- Map load time target: < 3s desktop, < 5s mobile
- Memory budget: < 50 MB for map + sprites

## Engine-Specific Implementation
*Technical approach for Phaser.js 3 + Next.js 16*

### Phaser Scene Structure
```
BootScene -> loads core assets (tilesheets, player spritesheet)
  |
  v
TownScene -> creates tilemap, spawns player, starts input
```

### Key Phaser APIs
- `this.make.tilemap({ key: 'town-map' })` -- Load Tiled JSON
- `map.createLayer('ground', tileset)` -- Create tile layers
- `this.physics.add.collider(player, collisionLayer)` -- Arcade collision
- `this.cameras.main.startFollow(player, true, 0.1, 0.1)` -- Camera follow with lerp
- `this.cameras.main.setDeadzone(48, 48)` -- Camera deadzone

### Next.js Integration
- Phaser game loaded via `dynamic(() => import('./PhaserGame'), { ssr: false })`
- Game component mounted on `/play` route
- React state NOT used for game state (Phaser manages its own state)
- React UI overlay (HUD, menus) rendered as absolute-positioned elements over the Phaser canvas

### Platform Adaptations
- Desktop: Full keyboard input, Phaser.Scale.FIT at 3x-4x scale
- Mobile: Touch input via virtual joystick, Phaser.Scale.FIT fills viewport, fullscreen API on tap

## Testing Plan
### Unit Tests
- [ ] Player moves correct distance per frame at configured speed
- [ ] Direction changes update sprite animation correctly
- [ ] Collision detection prevents movement into blocked tiles
- [ ] Camera position tracks player with configured deadzone and lerp

### Integration Tests
- [ ] Tiled JSON map loads without errors across all tile layers
- [ ] Phaser game initializes within Next.js without SSR conflicts
- [ ] Input events correctly map to player movement

### Player Tests
- [ ] Movement feels responsive and natural on desktop (keyboard)
- [ ] Movement feels responsive and natural on mobile (joystick)
- [ ] Map art looks correct with no tile seams or rendering artifacts
- [ ] Camera behavior feels smooth and non-disorienting

### Performance Tests
- [ ] Desktop Chrome: 60 FPS sustained for 5 minutes of continuous movement
- [ ] Mobile Chrome: 30 FPS sustained for 5 minutes of continuous movement
- [ ] Map load time < 3 seconds on desktop, < 5 seconds on mobile
- [ ] Memory usage < 50 MB after 10 minutes of play

## Implementation Notes
### Development Approach
1. Set up Phaser.js in Next.js with dynamic import (Day 1-2)
2. Create BootScene with asset loading (Day 2)
3. Create test tilemap in Tiled with all layers (Day 3)
4. Implement TownScene with tilemap rendering (Day 3-4)
5. Implement Player entity with movement and animation (Day 5-6)
6. Implement collision with collision layer (Day 6-7)
7. Implement camera system (Day 7)
8. Add mobile virtual joystick (Day 8)
9. Performance optimization and testing (Day 9-10)

### Potential Risks
- **Phaser + Next.js SSR conflict**: Mitigated by dynamic import with ssr: false
- **Tile seams on non-integer scale**: Mitigated by pixelArt: true and roundPixels: true
- **Mobile performance**: Mitigated by simpler rendering (fewer layers, smaller viewport)

### Future Considerations
- M0.2 will add multiplayer (other player sprites on same map)
- M0.3 will add NPC sprites and interaction zones
- Tilemap will expand from prototype 64x64 to full town map in Phase 1
